pages.full_text:
the boys, Julius, Victorian statue replaced. little water channel came in to Great Bath from the Sacred Spring (Aquae Sulis, & Minerva) & you could walk over it on a little bridge. Floor mosaics. Head of Minerva. Original cooler bath built too low, flooded by Avon, so Romans built a higher floor & used rooms as steam rooms. The Benedictines built the King's Bath above the sacred spring.
    Hot sunshine to end holiday. We visited Pouteney Bridge.

March 4th.
We've come to __Hamperley__, north of Ludlow. Simon got feasts for everyone out of the freezer when we arrived. Very hot sunny day.
    We've brough the old red push-truck for Charles to push – he's just got his first pair of size 3 shoes, – to try to help him stay upright.
pages.notes: Page continues from previous spread — the text begins mid-sentence about 'the boys, Julius...' relating to a visit to the Roman Baths in Bath. 'Pouteney Bridge' likely = Pulteney Bridge. 'Hamperley' underlined — possibly a hamlet near Ludlow; could also read as 'Hamperley' or [?Hamperley]. No year visible on this page; volume covers 1989–2003. The blue asterisks (***) visible above 'Hamperley' appear to be a decorative or rating mark in blue ink.

pages.full_text:
Charles + John are thrilled to see the [cont.]
    Jini, Margaret + Helen arrived at 9.30. the next morning. They overshot the end of the road although Thomas waved frantically. Margaret + Helen have taught Charles to push the little red truck along, wearing his first pair of shoes.
    After coffee we set of up the Long Mynd. Many trees have blown over. We walked 5 miles, + up and down 700 ft. Simon carried Charles in the pack. The rest of us held John's hands, + sometimes gave him piggy packs. He managed very well.
    We had a picnic lunch watching the gliders being launched by a long [continues]
pages.notes: Text is cut off at bottom — 'being launched by a long' continues onto the next page. 'Jini' may be a name (possibly 'Jini' or [?Jini]). 'Long Mynd' is a hill/ridge in Shropshire near Ludlow, consistent with the Hamperley location mentioned on the left page. 'set of' is as written (likely means 'set off'). The word '[cont.]' at top refers to the left-page gutter text bleeding in.
